items.html:
This course teaches the theory only

It is important to be clear from the outset: this course gives you the knowledge element of Basic Life Support only. Reading about chest compressions is not the same as being able to do them well under pressure, and you cannot become competent at CPR by learning online.

Before you perform CPR on a real person, you must also complete practical, hands-on training and assessment on a manikin, supervised by a qualified trainer and in line with Resuscitation Council UK guidance. That practical session lets you feel the right depth and rate, practise opening the airway, and get feedback you simply cannot get from a screen.

Keeping your skills current

Resuscitation skills fade surprisingly quickly — often within months of training. This is why your employer will arrange regular refresher sessions, usually every year, so that your knowledge and your hands-on ability both stay sharp.

  • Attend refreshers: keep your practical training up to date and never let it lapse.
  • Know your limits: only give the care you are trained and confident to give.
  • Practise the theory: use this course to prepare for and reinforce your hands-on sessions.
  • Ask questions: raise anything you are unsure about with your trainer.

Treat what follows as the foundation you build on in the training room, not a substitute for it.

items.question: Passing this online course on its own makes you fully competent to carry out CPR on a real person.
items.correctMsg: Correct — this is false. This course covers the knowledge only. You must also complete hands-on manikin training and assessment, in line with Resuscitation Council UK guidance, before you are competent to perform CPR.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite — this is false. Online theory cannot build the practical skill of CPR; you must also complete supervised manikin training and assessment before performing it for real.

items.html:
What cardiac arrest looks like

Cardiac arrest happens when the heart stops pumping blood properly. The person suddenly becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally. If you have checked for danger, tried to rouse them, opened the airway and then looked, listened and felt for no more than ten seconds, and breathing is absent or abnormal, treat it as cardiac arrest and start CPR.

In the first minutes, some people take slow, gasping, snoring or irregular breaths. These are called agonal breaths. They are a sign of cardiac arrest, not of recovery, and you must not let them fool you into waiting. If you are in any doubt, start CPR.

When it is a different emergency

Not every collapse is a cardiac arrest, and CPR is only for someone who is unresponsive and not breathing normally.

  • Breathing normally: if an unresponsive person is breathing normally, place them in the recovery position and call for help.
  • Fainting or a seizure: protect them from injury and check them once it passes; they usually keep breathing on their own.
  • Conscious and struggling: chest pain or breathlessness needs a 999 call, but not compressions while the heart is still beating.

You may also come across a valid DNACPR or ReSPECT decision that says CPR should not be attempted; you will look at these later. If you are ever unsure, phone 999 and the call handler will guide you.

items.question: You find a man collapsed and unresponsive. Over the next ten seconds he takes a few slow, noisy, irregular gasps. What does this most likely mean?

answers.text: These are agonal gasps, a sign of cardiac arrest — treat him as not breathing normally and start CPR.
answers.feedback: Correct. Occasional gasping breaths after a collapse are agonal breathing and point to cardiac arrest, so CPR should begin at once.

answers.text: He is breathing normally, so place him in the recovery position and monitor him.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Slow, irregular gasps are not normal breathing; treating them as recovery would fatally delay CPR.

answers.text: He is simply asleep, so leave him and check again in a few minutes.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. An unresponsive person who only gasps is in cardiac arrest; waiting would cost vital minutes.

answers.text: He is choking, so give five back blows straight away.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. There is nothing here to suggest a blocked airway; unresponsive plus abnormal breathing means start CPR.

items.html:
What good CPR looks like

Chest compressions keep a trickle of oxygen-rich blood moving to the brain and heart, so their quality matters enormously. For an adult, aim to:

  • Push hard: press down about 5 to 6 cm — roughly a third of the depth of the chest.
  • Push fast: at a rate of 100 to 120 compressions a minute.
  • Let the chest rise: allow it to come back up fully between each push without leaning on it.
  • Combine with breaths: give 30 compressions to 2 rescue breaths if you are trained and willing.

Every pause lets the blood flow drop away, so keep any interruptions as short as you possibly can.

Sharing the effort

Good CPR is genuinely tiring, and tired rescuers push more shallowly and slowly without realising it. Whenever a second trained person is available, share the work.

  • Swap roughly every two minutes: change over before fatigue sets in.
  • Keep pauses tiny: plan the swap so compressions stop for barely a second.
  • Coach each other: count out loud and give honest feedback on depth and rate.
  • Take turns with the AED: one person can fetch and set it up while the other keeps compressing.

Working smoothly as a team keeps compressions strong from the very first minute until the ambulance crew take over.

items.question: You and a colleague are giving CPR. After a couple of minutes you feel your compressions getting shallower and slower. What is the best action?

answers.text: Swap over quickly with your colleague, keeping any pause in compressions as short as possible.
answers.feedback: Correct. Swapping about every two minutes keeps compressions strong, and a slick change-over avoids a long, harmful pause.

answers.text: Carry on alone until the ambulance arrives so the count is not lost.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Pushing on while tired means shallow, slow compressions; sharing the effort keeps quality high.

answers.text: Stop CPR completely for a minute to rest, then start again.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. A long pause lets blood flow to the brain collapse; you should swap over rather than stop.

answers.text: Push faster than 120 a minute to make up for being tired.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Going faster than 120 reduces the depth and effectiveness of each compression; swap instead.

items.html:
Attaching an AED

An automated external defibrillator (AED) analyses the heart rhythm and, if it is needed, delivers a controlled electric shock to help the heart re-establish a normal beat. It is designed for untrained members of the public, so you cannot easily go wrong: turn it on and do exactly what it tells you.

Bare the chest and attach the sticky pads to clean, dry skin exactly as shown on the pictures — one below the right collarbone, the other on the left side below the armpit. If a second rescuer is there, keep chest compressions going while the pads are being put on.

Staying safe and special situations

The AED will tell everyone to stand clear while it analyses the rhythm and while it shocks. Nobody must be touching the casualty at that moment.

  • Dry the chest: wipe off sweat or water so the pads stick and the shock is not spread across wet skin.
  • Move hazards: keep pads clear of jewellery, medication patches and any implanted pacemaker lump.
  • Oxygen away: move any oxygen mask away from the chest before a shock is given.
  • Children: for a child use paediatric pads or a paediatric setting if available, but never delay using a standard AED if that is all you have.

Follow the voice prompts and get back to compressions the instant you are told to.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete the sentences about using an AED safely.
items.textField: An AED will only deliver a *shock* if the heart rhythm actually needs one. Attach the *pads* to the casualty’s bare, dry chest exactly as shown in the pictures. Then simply follow the spoken *prompts* the machine gives you. Before any shock, make sure that nobody is *touching* the casualty. Keep giving good chest compressions and only *pause* them when the AED tells you to.

items.html:
Handing over to the ambulance crew

Keep going until professional help takes over, the person shows clear signs of life such as normal breathing, or you become too exhausted to continue. When the ambulance crew arrive, do not simply stop — give them a quick, clear handover and carry on with CPR until they are ready to take charge.

Tell them what happened, roughly when the person collapsed, what you have done so far, how many shocks the AED has advised, and whether there is any DNACPR or ReSPECT form. If the person recovers, place them in the recovery position and keep a close watch on their breathing.

Afterwards, and looking after yourself

Being part of a resuscitation attempt is intense, whatever the outcome. Make sure the event is recorded accurately, and take part in any debrief your service offers.

  • Your wellbeing matters: it is normal to feel shaken; talk to your manager, a trusted colleague or an employee assistance scheme.
  • Respect end-of-life decisions: a valid DNACPR or ReSPECT record exists to prevent unwanted CPR, not to deny someone care.

Finally, remember the most important point of all: this course has given you the theory only. Before you perform CPR for real, you must complete practical, hands-on manikin training and assessment, in line with Resuscitation Council UK guidance.

items.question: The ambulance crew arrive while you are doing CPR on a resident. What is the most helpful thing to do?

answers.text: Give a clear handover — what happened, when she collapsed, what you have done and any DNACPR or ReSPECT information — and keep going until they take over.
answers.feedback: Correct. A brief, clear handover while you continue CPR gives the crew what they need without any harmful break in compressions.

answers.text: Stop immediately and step well back so they can take charge.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Stopping abruptly leaves a gap in compressions; keep going and hand over as they get ready.

answers.text: Leave the room to start writing your notes while they work.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. The crew need your account of what happened; recording can wait until after the handover.

answers.text: Say nothing so that you do not get in their way.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Staying silent hides vital information; a short, clear handover genuinely helps them.
